judgments.text: Patients receiving treatment_midostaurin have different objective_response rates compared to those not receiving treatment_midostaurin.
judgments.rationale: Midostaurin is an established AML therapy, especially in FLT3-mutated disease, and differences in response by receipt of this treatment are standard clinical knowledge rather than a novel hypothesis.

judgments.text: Patients receiving treatment_gilteritinib have different objective_response rates compared to those not receiving treatment_gilteritinib.
judgments.rationale: Gilteritinib is an established targeted AML therapy for FLT3-mutated relapsed or refractory disease, so treatment-associated response differences are not novel.

judgments.text: Patients receiving treatment_ivosidenib have different objective_response rates compared to those not receiving treatment_ivosidenib.
judgments.rationale: Ivosidenib is an established IDH1-targeted AML therapy, making response differences by treatment receipt a known clinical association.

judgments.text: Patients receiving treatment_enasidenib have different objective_response rates compared to those not receiving treatment_enasidenib.
judgments.rationale: Enasidenib is an established IDH2-targeted AML therapy, so response differences by treatment receipt are within standard AML treatment knowledge.

judgments.text: Patients receiving treatment_venetoclax_azacitidine have different objective_response rates compared to those not receiving treatment_venetoclax_azacitidine.
judgments.rationale: Venetoclax plus azacitidine is a standard lower-intensity AML regimen with well-established response benefit in older or intensive-therapy-ineligible patients.

judgments.text: Patients receiving treatment_7plus3 have different objective_response rates compared to those not receiving treatment_7plus3.
judgments.rationale: 7+3 induction chemotherapy is the canonical intensive AML regimen, so differences in objective response by its receipt are not novel.

judgments.text: The effect of treatment_venetoclax_azacitidine on objective_response differs by age_years.
judgments.rationale: A continuous age-dependent modification of venetoclax-azacitidine response is a subgroup-interaction claim beyond the basic guideline indication for older or unfit AML patients.

judgments.text: The effect of treatment_venetoclax_azacitidine on objective_response differs by ecog_ps.
judgments.rationale: Differential venetoclax-azacitidine treatment effect by ECOG performance status is a specific subgroup-effect claim not usually stated as a settled AML paradigm.

judgments.text: The effect of treatment_venetoclax_azacitidine on objective_response differs by unfit_for_intensive.
judgments.rationale: Use of venetoclax-azacitidine in patients unfit for intensive chemotherapy is central to its standard AML indication, so differential response by intensive-fitness status is not novel.

judgments.text: The effect of treatment_venetoclax_azacitidine on objective_response differs by tp53_mutation.
judgments.rationale: TP53-mutated AML is widely recognized as having poor outcomes and attenuated benefit with venetoclax-azacitidine, making this association established rather than novel.

judgments.text: The effect of treatment_7plus3 on objective_response differs by age_years.
judgments.rationale: Age is an established determinant of tolerance and response to intensive 7+3 AML induction, so this is standard clinical knowledge.

judgments.text: The effect of treatment_7plus3 on objective_response differs by ecog_ps.
judgments.rationale: ECOG performance status is a standard predictor of intensive chemotherapy fitness and outcome in AML.

judgments.text: The effect of treatment_7plus3 on objective_response differs by unfit_for_intensive.
judgments.rationale: Fitness for intensive induction is a core treatment-selection criterion for 7+3 in AML and not a novel modifier.

judgments.text: The effect of treatment_7plus3 on objective_response differs by tp53_mutation.
judgments.rationale: TP53 mutation is a well-established adverse-risk AML feature associated with poor response to intensive induction.

judgments.text: Venetoclax_azacitidine efficacy varies by age_years.
judgments.rationale: Venetoclax plus azacitidine is specifically studied and used in older AML populations, making age-related efficacy a standard consideration.

judgments.text: Venetoclax_azacitidine efficacy varies by ecog_ps.
judgments.rationale: Performance status is a conventional clinical factor in assessing venetoclax-azacitidine outcomes in older or unfit AML patients.

judgments.text: Venetoclax_azacitidine efficacy varies by unfit_for_intensive.
judgments.rationale: Unfitness for intensive chemotherapy is central to the indication and evidence base for venetoclax-azacitidine in AML.

judgments.text: Venetoclax_azacitidine efficacy varies by tp53_mutation.
judgments.rationale: TP53-mutated AML has well-known poor outcomes and distinct response patterns with venetoclax-azacitidine.

judgments.text: Venetoclax_azacitidine efficacy varies by idh1_mutation.
judgments.rationale: IDH1-mutated AML is a recognized molecular subgroup with documented sensitivity to venetoclax-based azacitidine regimens.

judgments.text: Venetoclax_azacitidine efficacy varies by idh2_mutation.
judgments.rationale: IDH2-mutated AML is a recognized molecular subgroup with documented sensitivity to venetoclax-based azacitidine regimens.

judgments.text: Venetoclax_azacitidine effect differs in patients aged <50.
judgments.rationale: Age is an established clinical stratifier for venetoclax-azacitidine use in AML, especially because the regimen is standard in older or intensive-chemotherapy-ineligible patients.

judgments.text: Venetoclax_azacitidine effect differs in patients aged 50-60.
judgments.rationale: Age-based heterogeneity of venetoclax-azacitidine benefit is a standard AML clinical consideration rather than a new functional-genomics claim.

judgments.text: Venetoclax_azacitidine effect differs in patients aged 60-70.
judgments.rationale: The 60-70 age range falls within commonly discussed AML fitness and treatment-selection strata for venetoclax-azacitidine.

judgments.text: Venetoclax_azacitidine effect differs in patients aged >70.
judgments.rationale: Patients over 70 are a canonical population for venetoclax-azacitidine in AML, so age-related effect differences here are established.

judgments.text: Venetoclax_azacitidine effect differs between fit and unfit patients.
judgments.rationale: Fitness or intensive-chemotherapy eligibility is central to established venetoclax-azacitidine treatment selection in AML.

judgments.text: Venetoclax_azacitidine effect differs by TP53 mutation status.
judgments.rationale: TP53-mutated AML is a well-known adverse-risk subgroup with recognized differential outcomes on venetoclax-azacitidine.

judgments.text: Venetoclax_azacitidine effect differs by ECOG performance status.
judgments.rationale: ECOG performance status is a standard clinical stratifier for AML treatment tolerability and venetoclax-azacitidine eligibility.

judgments.text: Venetoclax_azacitidine effect differs by secondary AML status.
judgments.rationale: Secondary AML status is a routinely recognized AML subgroup in venetoclax-azacitidine studies and clinical interpretation.

judgments.text: Venetoclax_azacitidine effect differs by complex karyotype status.
judgments.rationale: Complex karyotype is an established adverse-risk AML feature often linked with TP53 mutation and poorer venetoclax-azacitidine outcomes.

judgments.text: Venetoclax_azacitidine shows strongest effect in fit patients without TP53 mutation.
judgments.rationale: The combined claim that the strongest effect occurs specifically in fit patients without TP53 mutation is a subgroup-conditional interaction beyond standard guideline-level consensus.

judgments.text: Patients receiving treatment_cetuximab have different mean pfs_months compared to those not receiving treatment_cetuximab.
judgments.rationale: Cetuximab is an established colorectal cancer therapy with well-known effects on progression outcomes in molecularly selected metastatic disease.

judgments.text: Patients receiving treatment_bevacizumab have different mean pfs_months compared to those not receiving treatment_bevacizumab.
judgments.rationale: Bevacizumab effects on progression-free survival are a standard established association in colorectal cancer treatment references.

judgments.text: Patients receiving treatment_pembrolizumab have different mean pfs_months compared to those not receiving treatment_pembrolizumab.
judgments.rationale: Pembrolizumab benefit on progression outcomes in MSI-high or mismatch-repair-deficient colorectal cancer is established guideline-level knowledge.

judgments.text: Patients receiving treatment_encorafenib have different mean pfs_months compared to those not receiving treatment_encorafenib.
judgments.rationale: Encorafenib-based therapy is an established targeted treatment paradigm for BRAF V600E-mutant metastatic colorectal cancer with known progression outcome relevance.

judgments.text: Patients receiving treatment_trastuzumab_tucatinib have different mean pfs_months compared to those not receiving treatment_trastuzumab_tucatinib.
judgments.rationale: HER2-targeted trastuzumab plus tucatinib is an established treatment strategy for HER2-positive metastatic colorectal cancer with expected progression outcome relevance.

judgments.text: Patients receiving treatment_regorafenib have different mean pfs_months compared to those not receiving treatment_regorafenib.
judgments.rationale: Regorafenib is an established later-line colorectal cancer therapy with known effects on progression-related outcomes.

judgments.text: The effect of treatment_cetuximab on pfs_months differs by msi_high status.
judgments.rationale: An MSI-high-specific modification of cetuximab effect is a subgroup interaction claim beyond the standard anti-EGFR selection paradigm.

judgments.text: The effect of treatment_bevacizumab on pfs_months differs by msi_high status.
judgments.rationale: An MSI-high-specific modification of bevacizumab effect is not a standard guideline-level biomarker association and would be a subgroup-conditional claim.

judgments.text: The effect of treatment_pembrolizumab on pfs_months differs by msi_high status.
judgments.rationale: Differential pembrolizumab benefit by MSI-high status is the canonical established biomarker-treatment relationship in colorectal cancer.

judgments.text: Patients with stage_iv=1 receiving treatment_cetuximab have different pfs_months compared to stage_iv=0 patients receiving treatment_cetuximab.
judgments.rationale: Stage IV status as a prognostic clinical factor for progression outcomes among treated colorectal cancer patients is standard clinical knowledge rather than a novel mechanism or subgroup claim.

judgments.text: Patients with stage_iv=1 receiving treatment_pembrolizumab have different pfs_months compared to stage_iv=0 patients receiving treatment_pembrolizumab.
judgments.rationale: Stage IV versus non-stage IV status is a standard prognostic distinction for progression outcomes and would not be novel even within pembrolizumab-treated patients.

judgments.text: Patients with stage_iv=1 receiving treatment_bevacizumab have different pfs_months compared to stage_iv=0 patients receiving treatment_bevacizumab.
judgments.rationale: Stage IV disease is an established adverse prognostic feature, so different PFS among bevacizumab-treated patients by stage is not novel.

judgments.text: The effect of treatment_cetuximab on pfs_months differs by kras_mutation status.
judgments.rationale: KRAS mutation as a predictor of lack of benefit from cetuximab or other anti-EGFR therapy is established guideline-level oncology knowledge.

judgments.text: The effect of treatment_cetuximab on pfs_months differs by nras_mutation status.
judgments.rationale: NRAS mutation status is part of established extended RAS testing used to predict lack of benefit from cetuximab in colorectal cancer.

judgments.text: The effect of treatment_cetuximab on pfs_months differs by age_years (younger vs older patients).
judgments.rationale: Age-dependent modification of cetuximab PFS benefit is a subgroup-conditional claim not established as a standard predictive biomarker.

judgments.text: The effect of treatment_pembrolizumab on pfs_months differs by age_years (younger vs older patients).
judgments.rationale: Age-dependent modification of pembrolizumab PFS benefit is not a guideline-level predictive paradigm and would be considered exploratory.

judgments.text: The effect of treatment_cetuximab on pfs_months differs by ecog_ps status.
judgments.rationale: Although ECOG performance status is a standard prognostic and treatment-selection factor, a specific interaction with cetuximab benefit is not established consensus.

judgments.text: The effect of treatment_pembrolizumab on pfs_months differs by ecog_ps status.
judgments.rationale: A specific ECOG-dependent difference in pembrolizumab PFS benefit goes beyond the standard use of performance status as a general prognostic factor.

judgments.text: The effect of treatment_bevacizumab on pfs_months differs by ecog_ps status.
judgments.rationale: A specific ECOG-dependent modification of bevacizumab treatment effect is not a standard biomarker or guideline-level association.

judgments.text: The effect of treatment_cetuximab on pfs_months differs by nlr (high vs low).
judgments.rationale: NLR as a modifier of cetuximab benefit is an exploratory inflammatory-biomarker hypothesis rather than established oncology consensus.

judgments.text: The effect of treatment_pembrolizumab on pfs_months differs by nlr (high vs low).
judgments.rationale: NLR is an inflammatory/prognostic biomarker studied with checkpoint inhibitors, but it is not an established guideline-level modifier of pembrolizumab PFS benefit.

judgments.text: The effect of treatment_trastuzumab_tucatinib on pfs_months differs by her2_amplified status.
judgments.rationale: HER2 amplification/positivity is the canonical selection biomarker for trastuzumab- and tucatinib-based anti-HER2 therapy.

judgments.text: The effect of treatment_bevacizumab on pfs_months differs by her2_amplified status.
judgments.rationale: HER2 amplification is not an established predictive biomarker for bevacizumab benefit, so a HER2-conditional bevacizumab PFS effect is a nonstandard claim.

judgments.text: The effect of treatment_cetuximab on pfs_months differs by albumin_g_dl (high vs low).
judgments.rationale: Albumin is generally a prognostic or frailty/nutrition marker, not an established modifier of cetuximab treatment benefit.

judgments.text: The effect of treatment_pembrolizumab on pfs_months differs by albumin_g_dl (high vs low).
judgments.rationale: Albumin is not a standard predictive biomarker for pembrolizumab efficacy, making this subgroup treatment-effect claim non-consensus.

judgments.text: The effect of treatment_bevacizumab on pfs_months differs by albumin_g_dl (high vs low).
judgments.rationale: Albumin-based modification of bevacizumab PFS benefit is not a standard oncology treatment-selection paradigm.

judgments.text: The effect of treatment_cetuximab on pfs_months is strongest in patients with msi_high=-0.05 vs -0.05 without, suggesting msi_high modifies treatment effect.
judgments.rationale: MSI status is not a standard positive predictor for cetuximab benefit, and this malformed subgroup contrast would still be a speculative non-consensus modifier claim.

judgments.rationale: Enzalutamide is an established prostate cancer therapy with guideline-recognized clinical activity, so a generic response-rate difference is not novel.
